function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Specifically, how are directors elected - does Class B vote as a separate class to elect a majority of directors? How are the remaining directors elected? results.content:
# Voting Rights of Class A and Class B Common Stock in the SpaceX S-1

## 1. Voting Power Per Share

The filing establishes a dual-class structure with asymmetric voting power:

> "Subject to the terms of our charter, each holder of our Class A common stock is entitled to **one vote per share**; each holder of our Class B common stock is entitled to **ten votes per share**; and the holders of our Class C common stock will have **no voting rights**."

---

## 2. Election of Directors — Class B Elects a Majority (51%)

Yes, Class B votes **as a separate class** to elect a majority of the board. The exact language states:

> "With respect to the election of directors, our charter will provide that (i) holders of our Class B common stock, **voting separately as a class**, are entitled to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors** (rounded up to the nearest whole number) **for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding**; and that (ii) holders of all classes of our voting common stock, voting together as a single class, are entitled to elect the remaining directors (the '**Common Stock Directors**')."

This is reiterated in the controlled-company disclosure:

> "Under the terms of our charter, the holders of our outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, will have the right to elect 51% of the total number of authorized directors, rounded up to the nearest whole number (the '**Class B Directors**')."

---

## 3. Election of Remaining Directors

The remaining directors (the "Common Stock Directors") are elected by **all classes of voting common stock voting together as a single class**:

> "Holders of Class A and Class B common stock, **voting together as a single class**, will elect the remaining members of our board (the 'Common Stock Directors')."

---

## 4. Removal and Vacancy Filling

### Class B Directors

> "Class B Directors may be removed **with or without cause** by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, **voting separately as a class**. Vacancies occurring with respect to the Class B Directors, including as a result of newly created directorships on the board, may be filled at any time by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, **or by the remaining Class B Directors, and not any other persons**, subject to the terms of our charter."

### Common Stock Directors

> "Common Stock Directors may be removed with or without cause by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of voting common stock, **voting together as a single class**. Vacancies occurring with respect to the Common Stock Directors, including as a result of newly created directorships on the board, may be filled at any time by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of voting common stock, voting together as a single class, **or by the remaining directors**."

---

## 5. Voting on General Shareholder Matters

On matters other than director elections and the protective provisions described below, **both classes vote together as a single class**:

> "Generally speaking, with respect to matters to be voted on by shareholders of the Company, the holders of all classes of our voting common stock will **vote together as a single class**."

The cover page footnote confirms:

> "Class A shareholders and Class B shareholders will vote together as a single class on all matters to be voted on by shareholders under our charter, **except** the holders of our Class B common stock will have the right to elect a majority of our board and have certain other voting rights as a class."

---

## 6. Class B Protective Provisions (Separate-Class Votes)

Beyond director elections, Class B also has separate-class voting rights on certain fundamental corporate actions:

> "In addition to any other required vote, under our charter, the approval of the Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, is required to approve (1) any amendment to our charter that would make any change in the rights, powers, preferences and privileges of the Class B common stock (including with respect to Class B Directors); and (2) certain combinations, mergers or sales, as described in our charter."

The filing further notes an **opt-out** from statutory class votes:

> "Otherwise, classes of common stock will not be entitled to any separate class votes, as our charter will provide for an **opt-out from class votes** that would otherwise be required under the [TBOC]."

---

## 7. Elon Musk's Protected Leadership Roles

The filing grants Musk special removal protection tied to Class B approval:

> "Mr. Musk will only be subject to removal from the board and from his Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the board leadership positions with the approval of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of our Class B common stock, **voting separately as a class**."

---

## 8. Preferred Stock Precedence

All voting rights are subject to future preferred stock grants:

> "Notwithstanding the above, each of the voting rights described above will be subject to the rights that may be granted in the future to the holders of any one or more series of preferred stock, as applicable."

---

## Summary Table

| Feature | Class A | Class B | Class C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Votes per share** | 1 | 10 | 0 |
| **General matters** | Vote together with Class B as single class | Vote together with Class A as single class | No voting rights |
| **Director elections** | Vote with all classes to elect remaining ("Common Stock Directors") | **Vote separately as a class to elect 51% of directors ("Class B Directors")** | No voting rights |
| **Removal of Class B Directors** | No separate right | Majority of Class B, voting separately as a class | N/A |
| **Removal of Common Stock Directors** | Majority of voting common stock, voting together as single class | Participates in single-class vote | N/A |
| **Charter amendments affecting Class B rights** | No separate class vote | **Separate class approval required** | N/A |
| **Certain mergers/combinations** | No separate class vote | **Separate class approval required** | N/A |

steps.reasoning_content:
I now have a comprehensive understanding of the SpaceX dual-class voting structure. Let me analyze the question carefully:

The question asks me to:
1. Explain precisely how this dual-mechanism structure differs economically and practically from a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure in which all directors are elected by the combined class vote.
2. What is the specific implication for a Class A shareholder's ability to influence board composition even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of economic interest?

Let me think through this carefully:

**SpaceX's Structure (Dual-Mechanism):**
- Class A: 1 vote per share
- Class B: 10 votes per share
- Class C: 0 votes
- Class B votes SEPARATELY AS A CLASS to elect 51% of directors (Class B Directors)
- Class A + Class B vote TOGETHER AS A SINGLE CLASS to elect the remaining 49% of directors (Common Stock Directors)
- General matters: All voting classes vote together as a single class

**Conventional 10-vote/1-vote Dual-Class Structure:**
- Class A: 1 vote per share
- Class B: 10 votes per share
- ALL directors elected by combined class vote (Class A + Class B voting together)
- General matters: All voting classes vote together as a single class

**Key Differences:**

1. **Economic Difference:**
   - In a conventional structure, the 10:1 voting ratio creates a "voting power premium" for Class B, but Class A's voting power is still proportional to (Class A shares × 1) / (Class A shares × 1 + Class B shares × 10). If Class A holds a majority of economic interest (i.e., majority of total shares), Class A could still potentially have meaningful voting influence if Class B's economic stake is small enough.
   - In SpaceX's structure, the 51% director election carve-out is INDEPENDENT of the 10:1 ratio. Even if Class B holds a tiny economic stake (say 1% of shares), Class B STILL elects 51% of directors. This is a "hard floor" of control that doesn't depend on the 10:1 ratio at all.

2. **Practical Difference:**
   - In a conventional structure, Class A's voting influence on director elections scales with its economic stake. If Class A holds 80% of economic interest and Class B holds 20%, then:
     - Class A voting power = 80% × 1 = 80
     - Class B voting power = 20% × 10 = 200
     - Class A's share of voting power = 80 / (80 + 200) = 28.6%
     - Class B's share of voting power = 200 / (80 + 200) = 71.4%
   - So Class B still controls the board, but Class A has 28.6% of the vote.
   - In SpaceX's structure, Class B elects 51% of directors REGARDLESS of economic stake. Class A only gets to vote on the remaining 49% of directors, and even then, Class A's vote is diluted by Class B's 10:1 advantage.
   - So if Class A holds 80% of economic interest and Class B holds 20%:
     - Class B elects 51% of directors outright (Class B Directors)
     - For the remaining 49% (Common Stock Directors), Class A voting power = 80, Class B voting power = 200, so Class A gets 28.6% of the vote on those 49% of seats
     - Class A's effective influence on the board = 0% (of 51%) + 28.6% (of 49%) = ~14% of total board seats
     - Class B's effective influence = 100% (of 51%) + 71.4% (of 49%) = ~86% of total board seats

3. **Implication for Class A Shareholder:**
   - Even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a MAJORITY of economic interest (e.g., 80% of shares), they CANNOT elect a majority of the board.
   - In fact, they cannot even elect a single Class B Director.
   - Their influence is limited to the Common Stock Director seats (49% of board), and even there, their voting power is diluted by the 10:1 ratio.
   - This means Class A's economic majority does NOT translate to board control or even meaningful board influence.
   - The structure creates a "decoupling" of economic ownership from board control that is more extreme than a conventional dual-class structure.

Let me also think about the "economic" difference more carefully:

In a conventional 10:1 dual-class structure, the voting power of Class B is a function of:
- The 10:1 ratio
- The relative economic stakes of Class A and Class B

If Class B holds X% of economic interest, Class B's voting power = (X × 10) / (X × 10 + (100-X) × 1) = 10X / (9X + 100)

For Class B to control 50%+ of voting power: 10X / (9X + 100) > 0.5 → 10X > 4.5X + 50 → 5.5X > 50 → X > 9.09%

So in a conventional structure, Class B needs to hold at least ~9.09% of economic interest to control the board (with 10:1 voting).

In SpaceX's structure, Class B controls 51% of the board REGARDLESS of economic stake. Even if Class B holds 0.1% of economic interest, Class B still elects 51% of directors.

This is a fundamentally different economic proposition:
- Conventional: Voting control is a function of economic stake × voting multiplier
- SpaceX: Voting control is a function of (a) a fixed 51% director carve-out + (b) economic stake × voting multiplier for the remaining 49%

The SpaceX structure is more akin to a "founder-controlled" structure where the founder's control is enshrined in the charter regardless of economic dilution.

Let me also think about the practical implications:

1. **Class A cannot remove Class B Directors**: Even if Class A holds 99% of economic interest, Class A cannot remove a single Class B Director. Only Class B shareholders (voting separately as a class) can remove Class B Directors.

2. **Class A cannot fill Class B Director vacancies**: Vacancies in Class B Director seats can only be filled by Class B shareholders or the remaining Class B Directors.

3. **Class A's influence on Common Stock Directors is diluted**: Even for the 49% of Common Stock Director seats, Class A's voting power is diluted by the 10:1 ratio. So if Class A holds 80% of economic interest and Class B holds 20%, Class A only has 28.6% of the vote on Common Stock Director elections.

4. **Class A cannot block charter amendments affecting Class B**: Class B has a separate class vote on charter amendments affecting Class B rights and certain mergers/combinations.

5. **Class A cannot remove Elon Musk**: Musk can only be removed with the approval of Class B shareholders voting separately as a class.

Let me also think about the "economic" difference in terms of valuation:

In a conventional dual-class structure, the voting premium of Class B is reflected in:
- A higher per-share value for Class B (due to control premium)
- A lower per-share value for Class A (due to lack of control)

In SpaceX's structure, the voting premium of Class B is even more extreme because:
- Class B has a guaranteed 51% board control regardless of economic stake
- This creates a much larger control premium for Class B
- And a much larger discount for Class A

The economic implication is that Class A shares are essentially "economic-only" shares with very limited governance rights, while Class B shares are "control" shares with both economic and governance rights.

Now let me think about the specific implication for a Class A shareholder's ability to influence board composition even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of economic interest:

Even if Class A shareholders collectively hold, say, 80% of economic interest:
1. They CANNOT elect any Class B Directors (51% of board) - this is reserved for Class B voting separately as a class
2. They CAN only vote on Common Stock Directors (49% of board), but their voting power is diluted by the 10:1 ratio
3. If Class B holds 20% of economic interest, Class A's voting power on Common Stock Directors = 80 / (80 + 200) = 28.6%
4. So Class A's effective influence on the board = 0% × 51% + 28.6% × 49% = ~14% of total board seats
5. Class A cannot elect a majority of the board even with an 80% economic majority

This is fundamentally different from a conventional dual-class structure where:
1. All directors are elected by combined class vote
2. Class A's voting power = (Class A shares × 1) / (Class A shares × 1 + Class B shares × 10)
3. If Class A holds 80% of economic interest and Class B holds 20%, Class A's voting power = 80 / (80 + 200) = 28.6% of total board seats
4. Class A still cannot elect a majority, but its influence is spread across ALL board seats, not just 49% of them

Wait, let me reconsider. In a conventional structure, Class A's voting power is 28.6% across ALL board seats. In SpaceX's structure, Class A's voting power is 28.6% across only 49% of board seats (and 0% across the other 51%).

So:
- Conventional: Class A influence = 28.6% × 100% = 28.6% of board
- SpaceX: Class A influence = 28.6% × 49% + 0% × 51% = 14.0% of board

So SpaceX's structure cuts Class A's board influence roughly in half compared to a conventional structure, given the same economic stakes.

Actually, let me reconsider this. The question is about "influence" not "control." Let me think about this more carefully.

In a conventional structure, Class A's voting power on director elections = 28.6% (if Class A holds 80% economic and Class B holds 20% with 10:1 voting). This means Class A can influence 28.6% of the vote on EACH director seat. But since Class B has 71.4% of the vote, Class B can elect ALL directors. So Class A's influence is essentially 0% in terms of actually electing directors (assuming Class B votes as a bloc).

Wait, but in a conventional structure, if directors are elected by plurality voting (which is typical), then Class B can elect all directors because it has majority voting power. So Class A's influence is 0% in terms of actually electing directors.

In SpaceX's structure, Class B elects 51% of directors outright (Class B Directors). For the remaining 49% (Common Stock Directors), Class B has 71.4% of the vote (if Class A holds 80% economic and Class B holds 20% with 10:1 voting). So Class B can also elect all Common Stock Directors. So Class A's influence is 0% in terms of actually electing directors.

Hmm, so in both structures, Class A's influence is 0% in terms of actually electing directors (assuming Class B votes as a bloc and plurality voting).

But the KEY difference is:

In a conventional structure, Class A's voting power is a FUNCTION of its economic stake. If Class A's economic stake grows large enough (relative to Class B's), Class A could eventually gain majority voting power and elect directors.

Specifically, in a conventional 10:1 structure, Class A needs to hold more than 90.91% of economic interest to have majority voting power (since Class B needs less than 9.09% to maintain control).

In SpaceX's structure, Class A CANNOT gain majority voting power on director elections NO MATTER HOW LARGE its economic stake is. Even if Class A holds 99.99% of economic interest, Class B still elects 51% of directors (as long as any Class B shares remain outstanding).

This is the KEY economic and practical difference:

- Conventional: Class A's voting power is bounded by its economic stake × voting multiplier. There is a theoretical threshold (90.91% economic interest) above which Class A gains control.
- SpaceX: Class A's voting power on director elections is HARD-CAPPED at 49% of board seats (and even that is subject to the 10:1 dilution). There is NO economic threshold above which Class A gains control.

This is a much more extreme form of founder control.

Let me also think about the "practical" difference:

In a conventional structure, Class A shareholders can:
- Vote on all director elections (but with diluted voting power)
- Potentially gain control if they accumulate enough economic interest
- Influence the board through voting power proportional to their economic stake × voting multiplier

In SpaceX's structure, Class A shareholders can:
- Vote only on Common Stock Director elections (49% of board)
- NEVER gain control of the board, no matter how much economic interest they accumulate
- Influence only 49% of the board, and even there with diluted voting power

The practical implication is that Class A shareholders in SpaceX have essentially NO path to board control through economic accumulation. Their only path to influence is through:
- Voting on Common Stock Director elections (49% of board, subject to 10:1 dilution)
- Voting on general shareholder matters (subject to 10:1 dilution)
- Negotiation with Class B shareholders (Musk)

This is a much weaker governance position than in a conventional dual-class structure.

Now let me also think about the "economic" difference in terms of share valuation:

In a conventional dual-class structure, the voting premium of Class B is reflected in:
- A higher per-share value for Class B (due to control premium)
- A lower per-share value for Class A (due to lack of control)

In SpaceX's structure, the voting premium of Class B is even more extreme because:
- Class B has a guaranteed 51% board control regardless of economic stake
- This creates a much larger control premium for Class B

## How SpaceX's Dual-Mechanism Voting Structure Differs from a Conventional 10-Vote/1-Vote Dual-Class Structure

### 1. The Two Structures Compared

**Conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure (all directors elected by combined class vote):**
- Class B = 10 votes/share; Class A = 1 vote/share.
- All directors are elected by Class A and Class B voting *together as a single class*, with each share weighted by its vote multiplier.
- Class B's control of the board is therefore *contingent and proportional*: it depends on Class B maintaining enough economic ownership relative to Class A so that 10× its stake exceeds Class A's stake.

**SpaceX's dual-mechanism structure (per the S-1 charter provisions):**
- Class B = 10 votes/share; Class A = 1 vote/share; Class C = 0 votes.
- **Mechanism (i):** Class B, *voting separately as a class*, elects **51% of the total authorized directors** (the "Class B Directors"), rounded up, "for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding."
- **Mechanism (ii):** Class A and Class B, *voting together as a single class*, elect the remaining directors (the "Common Stock Directors") and decide all general shareholder matters.
- Class B Directors may be removed only by a majority of Class B voting *separately as a class*; vacancies in Class B seats may be filled only by Class B or the remaining Class B Directors, "and not any other persons."
- Class B also has separate-class veto rights over charter amendments affecting Class B rights and over certain mergers/combinations, plus the right to approve removal of Musk as CEO/Chairman.

### 2. Economic Differences

**(a) Absolute vs. proportional board control.** In a conventional 10-vote structure, Class B's grip on the board is a *function of its economic stake*. Class A can mathematically overcome Class B's 10× multiplier if Class A accumulates more than ~90.9% of the economic interest (the breakeven at which 1 × f > 10 × (1 − f), i.e., f > 10/11). Above that threshold, Class A — despite the 10× disadvantage — could elect the entire board.

In SpaceX's structure, the 51% Class B Director block is **not a function of relative economic ownership at all**. The 10-vote multiplier is *irrelevant* to that 51% block; what matters is the separate-class election mechanism. So long as a single Class B share remains outstanding, Class B elects 51% of the board regardless of whether Class A holds 10%, 50%, 90%, or 99.99% of the economic interest. There is no economic threshold Class A can cross to capture those seats.

**(b) Immunity to dilution.** In a conventional structure, issuing additional Class A shares dilutes Class B's voting percentage and can eventually erode its board control. In SpaceX's structure, the 51% Class B Director block is **immune to dilution** — equity issuances of Class A (or Class C) cannot reduce Class B below 51% of the board so long as any Class B share is outstanding. The economic/voting decoupling is structural, not arithmetic.

**(c) Redundancy of the 10× multiplier for board control.** In SpaceX's design, the 10-vote-per-share feature does the work only for: (i) electing the 49% Common Stock Directors, (ii) general shareholder matters, and (iii) removal of Common Stock Directors. For the 51% Class B Director block, the 10× feature is essentially redundant — the separate-class election is what delivers control. This makes SpaceX's structure a *stronger* entrenchment device than a pure high-vote dual class, because it layers an absolute class-vote lock on top of the proportional 10× multiplier.

### 3. Practical Differences

| Dimension | Conventional 10-vote/1-vote (combined vote for all directors) | SpaceX dual-mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Class B board control | Proportional to economic stake × 10 | Absolute 51% block, independent of economic stake |
| Class A path to board majority | Theoretical: accumulate >~90.9% of economic interest | None, so long as any Class B share is outstanding |
| Effect of Class A dilution of Class B | Can erode Class B control | No effect on Class B's 51% block |
| Removal of Class B-affiliated directors | Combined class vote (subject to 10× weighting) | Class B only, voting separately as a class |
| Vacancy filling for Class B seats | Combined class vote | Class B or remaining Class B Directors only — "not any other persons" |
| Hostile-takeover immunity | Conditional (acquirer could cross the ~90.9% threshold) | Effectively absolute for the board majority |

### 4. Specific Implication for a Class A Shareholder Holding a Majority of Economic Interest

Even if Class A shareholders collectively held a **majority — indeed, even a supermajority — of the economic interest** in SpaceX, their ability to influence board composition would be **structurally capped at the 49% Common Stock Director block**. Specifically:

1. **No path to a board majority.** Class A cannot elect, remove, or replace any Class B Director under any circumstance while Class B shares remain outstanding. Class A's maximum achievable board representation is the Common Stock Directors (49% of the board, rounded down). A Class A economic majority therefore **cannot translate into board control** — the economic interest and board control are decoupled for the majority of seats.

2. **No leverage through accumulation.** Unlike a conventional 10-vote structure, where a Class A shareholder could in principle buy enough shares to outvote Class B on director elections, in SpaceX's structure **no amount of Class A accumulation changes the 51% Class B block**. The only route to influencing those seats is conversion/elimination of Class B shares, which itself requires Class B's separate-class consent (and is further protected by the Class B protective provisions on charter amendments and certain mergers).

3. **Asymmetric removal rights.** Class A cannot remove a Class B Director even with unanimous Class A support; only Class B, voting separately as a class, can do so. Conversely, Class B participates (with 10× weighting) in the combined-class vote to remove Common Stock Directors. So Class A's influence over its own 49% block is also subject to Class B's 10× voting power on the combined-class vote.

4. **Practical governance consequence.** A Class A shareholder with a majority of economic interest is, in board-composition terms, a **permanent minority**. Economic ownership confers no corresponding ability to determine — or even to influence through ordinary voting — the majority of the board. This is materially more entrenching than a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure, where economic majority (above the ~90.9% threshold) would at least theoretically confer full board control.

In short: SpaceX's structure converts what would otherwise be a *proportional, dilutable, threshold-dependent* founder lock (the conventional 10-vote model) into an *absolute, dilution-proof, threshold-free* lock on a board majority. For a Class A shareholder, holding a majority of the economic interest is **economically real but governance-irrelevant** with respect to the majority of the board — the separate-class election of the Class B Directors is the dispositive mechanism, not the 10-vote multiplier.

- Class B = 10 votes/share; Class A = 1 vote/share.
